David faces a three-year famine, and when he inquires of the Lord, the answer is stark: Saul's bloodguilt against the Gibeonites has brought judgment on the land. This is not a comfortable truth. Years of hunger have fallen on an entire nation because of a former king's zealous cruelty—a cruelty committed in the name of protecting Israel. The Gibeonites, whom Israel had once sworn to protect by covenant, became targets of Saul's ambition. Now David must answer for a sin that was not his own, yet for which his people pay the price. The famine reminds us that God takes seriously both the protection of the vulnerable and the keeping of oaths. When we assume our intentions justify breaking our word or harming those we've sworn to protect, we set in motion consequences that ripple far beyond our own generation.

David's response reveals something important about his kingship. He does not defend Saul's memory or argue with the Lord's verdict. Instead, he goes directly to the Gibeonites and asks what restitution is possible. Their demand is severe: seven of Saul's descendants must be hanged. David agrees and delivers them, sparing only Mephibosheth because of his covenant oath with Jonathan. Here we see justice enacted, but also the cost of justice. The execution of Saul's grandsons is not presented as satisfying or redemptive—it is presented as what must be done. Rizpah's vigil over the bodies, preventing birds and beasts from desecrating them, testifies to the weight of this moment. Her grief-stricken faithfulness—spreading sackcloth and standing guard through harvest season—confronts us with the human reality of judgment. These were not abstract principles being satisfied; they were descendants, sons, whose lives hung on ancient oaths and ancestral sins.

Yet the chapter does not end in despair. Only after justice is administered and the dead are honored with proper burial does the text tell us: "God answered their prayers for the land." The famine lifts. But notice what triggers this mercy: not David's cleverness or his military prowess, but his willingness to face hard truth and do what justice required, combined with his honoring of the dead. David then continues to fight the Philistines, nearly losing his life until Abishai saves him. His men refuse to let him risk himself again, speaking of David as "the lamp of Israel"—a reminder that God preserves His anointed not for David's sake alone, but for the sake of His people. The chapter closes with victories against Rapha's descendants, remnants of the old enemy, fallen one by one.

What David encounters in this chapter is the severe side of God's justice and the interconnected nature of covenant and consequence. The famine was neither random nor punishment for David's sins—it was the harvest of Saul's bloodguilt. Yet David's willingness to make restitution, to respect the oath Israel had made, and to honor even the dead opens the way for God's blessing to return. We are reminded that justice is real, that our actions have weight beyond our lifetime, and that sometimes obedience means accepting responsibility for wrongs we did not commit. Yet we are also reminded that when we turn toward God's justice rather than away from it, when we keep our word even when it costs us, the Lord does answer. The lamp of Israel continues to burn.
